Model Details.Model Description: M3-Embedding is a text embedding model distinguished by its versatility in Multi-Linguality, Multi-Functionality, and Multi-Granularity. It provides uniform support for semantic retrieval in over 100 languages. The model can simultaneously perform dense retrieval, multi-vector retrieval, and sparse retrieval. It is also capable of processing inputs of different granularities, from short sentences to long documents of up to 8,192 tokens. The model is based on an XLM-ROBERTa architecture and was trained using a novel self-knowledge distillation approach to integrate relevance scores from different retrieval functionalities, enhancing training quality. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 1]
Model Details.Model Version: The academic paper is the fourth version (v4) on arXiv. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 1] However, a specific version for the model itself is not mentioned.
Model Details.Model Date: The model's associated paper was published on arXiv on June 28, 2024. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 1]
Model Details.Model Type: M3-Embedding is a text embedding model based on the XLMRobertaModel architecture. [config.json]
Model Details.Paper or Other Resources: Chen, J., Xiao, S., Zhang, P., Luo, K., Lian, D., & Liu, Z. (2024). M3-Embedding: Multi-Linguality, Multi-Functionality, Multi-Granularity Text Embeddings Through Self-Knowledge Distillation. arXiv preprint arXiv:2402.03216v4. [Chen et al., 2024]
Model Details.License: The paper states that the model, code, and data are publicly available, but does not specify a license. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 1]
Model Details.Where to Send Questions: Questions can be directed to the authors of the academic paper via their listed emails: stxiao@baai.ac.cn, {namespace.pt, luokun695, zhengliu1026}@gmail.com, chenjianlv@mail.ustc.edu.cn, liandefu@ustc.edu.cn. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 1]

Intended Use.Primary Intended Uses: The primary intended use of M3-Embedding is for information retrieval (IR) tasks. It is designed to support multiple retrieval functionalities, including dense retrieval (using the [CLS] token embedding), sparse/lexical retrieval (using term weights derived from token embeddings), and multi-vector retrieval (using all output embeddings for fine-grained relevance). [Chen et al., 2024, p. 2, 4] The model is intended for multi-lingual and cross-lingual retrieval scenarios, as well as for handling documents of varying lengths up to 8,192 tokens. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 1]
Intended Use.Primary Intended Users: The primary users are researchers and developers in the fields of Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Information Retrieval (IR) who require versatile text embeddings for building and improving retrieval systems. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 1]
Intended Use.Out-of-Scope Uses: The model's performance on documents significantly exceeding the 8,192 token limit has not been investigated and could pose challenges in terms of computational resources and efficiency. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 9] The generalizability of the model to datasets with characteristics very different from its training data needs further investigation. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 9] Therefore, its use in contexts with extremely long documents or highly specialized, unseen domains should be approached with caution.

Factors.Relevant Factors: The model's performance is influenced by several factors, including the language of the text, the length of the input sequence, and the specific retrieval functionality (dense, sparse, multi-vector) being used. [Chen et al., 2024, Tables 1, 2, 3]
Factors.Evaluation Factors: The model was evaluated across a wide range of languages to test its multi-lingual and cross-lingual capabilities. [Chen et al., 2024, Tables 1, 2, 3] It was also evaluated on datasets with varying document lengths to assess its multi-granularity performance. [Chen et al., 2024, Table 3, Table 4]

Metrics.Model Performance Measures: The model's performance was primarily evaluated using standard information retrieval metrics. For multi-lingual and long-document retrieval on the MIRACL and MLDR benchmarks, the primary metric was nDCG@10 (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain at 10). [Chen et al., 2024, p. 6, 8] For cross-lingual retrieval on the MKQA benchmark, the primary metric was Recall@100. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 7]
Metrics.Decision Thresholds: Not applicable. As an embedding model, it produces representations of text; decision thresholds would be determined by the downstream application (e.g., a similarity score cutoff for retrieval).

Evaluation Data.Datasets:
The model was evaluated on several public benchmarks: 
- **MIRACL (Multilingual Information Retrieval Across a Continuum of Languages):** Used for evaluating multi-lingual ad-hoc retrieval performance in 18 languages. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 6]
- **MKQA (Multilingual Knowledge Questions & Answers):** Used for evaluating cross-lingual retrieval performance, with queries in 25 non-English languages retrieving from an English Wikipedia corpus. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 7]
- **MLDR (Multilingual Long-Doc Retrieval):** A benchmark curated from multilingual articles from Wikipedia, Wudao, and mC4, used to evaluate long-document retrieval. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 8]
- **NarrativeQA:** An English-only dataset used for evaluating long-document retrieval capabilities. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 8]
Evaluation Data.Motivation: The datasets were chosen to comprehensively evaluate the model's claimed versatility across its three key dimensions: multi-linguality (MIRACL), cross-linguality (MKQA), and multi-granularity, specifically long-document retrieval (MLDR, NarrativeQA). [Chen et al., 2024, p. 6-8]
Evaluation Data.Preprocessing: For evaluation, standard IR toolkits were used. For dense retrieval, embeddings were generated and indexed using Faiss. For sparse retrieval, term weights were generated and indexed using Lucene. The evaluation followed the official benchmark procedures. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 6] To ensure a fair comparison with the BM25 baseline, the same XLM-Roberta tokenizer used by M3-Embedding was applied to BM25. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 6]

Training Data.Datasets:
The model was trained in a multi-stage process using a large-scale, diverse, and multi-lingual dataset collected from three sources: 
1. **Unsupervised Data:** 1.2 billion text pairs were curated from various multi-lingual corpora, including Wikipedia, S2ORC, xP3, mC4, CC-News, and MTP. For cross-lingual learning, parallel sentences from NLLB and CCMatrix were used. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 3, Table 8]
2. **Fine-tuning Data (Labeled Corpora):** A collection of smaller, high-quality datasets were used for fine-tuning. This included 8 English datasets (e.g., MS MARCO, HotpotQA, NQ), 7 Chinese datasets (e.g., DuReader, mMARCO-ZH), and data for other languages from Mr. TyDi and MIRACL. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 3, Table 8]
3. **Synthetic Data:** To address the scarcity of long-document retrieval data, synthetic question-article pairs were generated. Lengthy articles were sampled from Wikipedia, Wudao, and mC4, and questions were generated using GPT-3.5. This dataset is referred to as MultiLongDoc. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 3, Table 8]
Training Data.Motivation: A large-scale and diverse multi-lingual dataset was required to achieve the model's goal of three-fold versatility (multi-linguality, multi-functionality, multi-granularity). The three data sources complement each other and were applied at different training stages to build a robust and versatile embedding model. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 2, 3]
Training Data.Preprocessing: The raw unsupervised data was filtered to remove potentially bad content and low-relevance samples. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 3] To prevent the model from relying only on initial sentences in long documents, a strategy of randomly shuffling text segments was applied to some passages during training. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 14]
Training Data.Labeling Functions: The fine-tuning data was sourced from existing human-labeled corpora. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 3] The synthetic data was generated by using GPT-3.5 with a specific prompt to create questions based on provided text passages. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 3, 14]

Quantitative Analyses.Unitary Results:
M3-Embedding demonstrates state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks. 
- On the **MLDR** test set for long-document retrieval, the full M3-Embedding model ('All') achieves an average nDCG@10 of 65.0, outperforming baselines like E5mistral-7b (42.6) and text-embedding-ada-002 (32.5). [Chen et al., 2024, Table 3]
- On the **MIRACL** dev set for multi-lingual retrieval, the 'All' model achieves an average nDCG@10 of 71.5, compared to baselines like mE5large (66.6) and E5mistral-7b (63.4). [Chen et al., 2024, Table 1]
- On the **MKQA** benchmark for cross-lingual retrieval, the 'All' model achieves an average Recall@100 of 75.5, surpassing baselines like mE5large (70.9) and E5mistral-7b (70.1). [Chen et al., 2024, Table 2]
Quantitative Analyses.Intersectional Results: The model's performance varies by language, but it maintains a consistent advantage over baselines in most individual languages. For example, in the MLDR benchmark, the 'All' model achieves the highest nDCG@10 score in all 14 tested languages, including Arabic (64.7), German (57.9), English (63.8), Spanish (86.8), and Chinese (40.0). [Chen et al., 2024, Table 3] In cross-lingual retrieval (MKQA), M3-Embedding shows particularly strong performance in low-resource languages like Khmer (km), where its Dense model scores 68.6 Recall@100, significantly outperforming prior models like mContriever (26.8). [Chen et al., 2024, Table 2]

Ethical Considerations.Data: The model was trained on multilingual data including a wide variety of languages. The authors note that due to the uneven distribution of training data across these languages, the model's performance may vary, which could potentially be seen as discriminatory or unfair. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 9]
Ethical Considerations.Mitigation Strategies: The authors state that their work conforms to the ACL Ethics Policy. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 9] However, specific strategies to mitigate performance disparities across languages are not detailed, beyond acknowledging the issue.
Ethical Considerations.Risks and Harms: The primary risk identified is the potential for performance disparities across different languages, which could lead to inequitable performance for users of less-represented languages. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 9] As with any language model, there is also a risk of perpetuating biases present in the 1.2 billion text pairs of training data.

Caveats and Recommendations.Caveats and Recommendations:
The authors provide several caveats: 
1. **Generalizability:** While the model performs well on established benchmarks, its generalizability to diverse, real-world datasets with different characteristics needs further investigation. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 9]
2. **Document Length:** The model is designed for inputs up to 8192 tokens. Processing extremely long documents beyond this limit could pose challenges for computational resources and model efficiency, and performance is not guaranteed. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 9]
3. **Language Variation:** The paper claims support for over 100 languages, but potential performance variations across this broad range of languages, language families, and linguistic characteristics have not been thoroughly discussed or evaluated. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 9]

Model Details.Model Architecture: The model uses an XLMRobertaModel architecture. Key parameters include: 24 hidden layers, a hidden size of 1024, 16 attention heads, an intermediate size of 4096, and a vocabulary size of 250002. The maximum position embedding length is 8194. [config.json] The model is used within a sentence-transformers framework that adds a pooling layer and a normalization layer after the transformer output. [modules.json]
Model Details.Compute Infrastructure: The model was trained on high-performance GPUs. The pre-training stage was conducted on 32 A100 (40GB) GPUs. The fine-tuning stage was conducted on 24 A800 (80GB) GPUs. [Chen et al., 2024, p. 14]
Model Details.Associated Libraries: The model was developed using PyTorch (version 2.1.2), transformers (version 4.33.0), and sentence-transformers (version 2.2.2). [config_sentence_transformers.json]

Citations.Citations: [Chen et al., 2024] Chen, J., Xiao, S., Zhang, P., Luo, K., Lian, D., & Liu, Z. (2024). M3-Embedding: Multi-Linguality, Multi-Functionality, Multi-Granularity Text Embeddings Through Self-Knowledge Distillation. arXiv preprint arXiv:2402.03216v4.
